name: Yurei
description: Yurei, literally translated as 'faint spirit,' are a class of Japanese ghosts. They are spirits prevented from a peaceful afterlife, typically due to a traumatic, violent, or unjust death, or because they are consumed by powerful emotions such as rage, sorrow, or a desire for revenge. Unlike Western ghosts, Yurei are often depicted as having a specific reason for remaining in the mortal realm, a lingering attachment or unfinished business that binds them to a particular location or person. They are not simply lost souls, but active participants in the world, driven by unresolved issues. Their appearance and behavior reflect the circumstances of their death and the strength of their emotions. A Yurei's ultimate goal is often to resolve the issue preventing their transcendence, though some are malevolent and seek only to inflict suffering on the living.
subtitle: Japanese Ghost
appearance: Typically depicted as pale, gaunt figures with long, unkempt black hair. They often wear white burial kimonos and lack legs, floating instead. A hitai-ita (forehead protector) is sometimes present, signifying their status as a deceased spirit. The appearance can vary based on the cause of death; for example, a murdered Yurei might have visible wounds.
habitat: Yurei are typically bound to specific locations associated with their death or the object of their lingering attachment. This could be a house, a forest, a roadside, or even a specific object.
behavior: Yurei are driven by their unresolved emotions. They may haunt a location, re-enacting the events of their death, or they may target specific individuals connected to their suffering. Their behavior ranges from mournful weeping and unsettling whispers to violent poltergeist activity and the spreading of curses. Some seek resolution and peace, while others are consumed by vengeance.

keyMyths.title: Oiwa Yotsuya Kaidan
keyMyths.description: Oiwa is a famous Yurei from a kabuki play. She was poisoned by her husband, Iemon, who wanted to marry a wealthier woman. Her vengeful spirit returns to torment Iemon, driving him to madness and causing him to kill his new wife and others. This story showcases the Yurei's power to inflict curses and exact revenge, highlighting the dangers of betrayal and injustice. Source: Yotsuya Kaidan play and various retellings.

keyMyths.title: The Lady in the Snow (Yuki-Onna)
keyMyths.description: Yuki-Onna is a snow spirit who appears as a beautiful woman, but she is deadly. She spares a young man's life on the condition that he never tells anyone he saw her. Years later, he breaks his promise, and she appears to him again, taking his life or the lives of his children before vanishing back into the snow. This highlights the Yurei's connection to nature and the consequences of broken promises. Source: Lafcadio Hearn's Kwaidan

keyMyths.title: The Teke Teke
keyMyths.description: A schoolgirl fell onto the train tracks and was cut in half by the train. Now a vengeful spirit, she crawls on her hands and elbows, making a 'teke teke' sound, and attacks anyone who crosses her path. She is a modern example of a Yurei born from a tragic accident, illustrating the enduring fear of sudden, violent death. Source: Urban Legend

sources.author: Lafcadio Hearn
sources.work: Kwaidan: Stories and Studies of Strange Things
sources.description: A collection of Japanese folk tales and ghost stories, providing valuable insights into traditional beliefs about Yurei.
sources.text: A collection of Japanese folk tales and ghost stories, providing valuable insights into traditional beliefs about Yurei.

sources.author: Ruth Benedict
sources.work: The Chrysanthemum and the Sword: Patterns of Japanese Culture
sources.description: Offers a cultural context for understanding the importance of honor, duty, and social harmony in Japanese society, which can contribute to the creation of Yurei.
sources.text: Offers a cultural context for understanding the importance of honor, duty, and social harmony in Japanese society, which can contribute to the creation of Yurei.

sources.author: Michiko Iwasaka and Barre Toelken
sources.work: Ghosts and the Japanese: Cultural Experience in Japanese Death Legends
sources.description: Examines the cultural significance of ghosts in Japanese society, exploring their role in maintaining social order and addressing anxieties about death and the afterlife.
sources.text: Examines the cultural significance of ghosts in Japanese society, exploring their role in maintaining social order and addressing anxieties about death and the afterlife.
